key_points: Sound is a form of energy that is produced due to vibration of objects and propagates as a wave.
Sound requires a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to travel; cannot travel in vacuum.
Speed of sound depends on the medium: fastest in solids, slower in liquids, slowest in gases.
Amplitude of vibration affects loudness; frequency affects pitch; quality/timbre depends on waveform.
Ultrasound: sound waves with frequency > 20,000 Hz; Infrasound: frequency < 20 Hz.
Echo is the reflection of sound; used in SONAR and echolocation.
Musical instruments produce sound by vibrating strings, air columns, or membranes.
formulas: Speed of sound: v = d / t, where d = distance, t = time taken
Frequency: f = number of vibrations / time
Wavelength: λ = v / f, where v = speed of sound, f = frequency
Time for echo: t = 2d / v, where d = distance of reflecting surface
examples: A tuning fork vibrating at 256 Hz produces a sound of pitch 256 Hz.
Speed of sound in air ≈ 343 m/s at 25°C; in water ≈ 1482 m/s; in steel ≈ 5100 m/s.
If sound travels 686 m in 2 s, speed v = d / t = 686 / 2 = 343 m/s.
An echo is heard 4 s after clapping; distance of reflecting surface = v × t / 2 = 343 × 4 / 2 = 686 m.
exam_tips: Remember sound cannot travel in vacuum.
Identify type of sound wave: longitudinal (all sound in air, water, solids).
Use echo formula correctly; divide total time by 2 to get one-way distance.
Understand pitch, loudness, and quality distinctions.
Revise practical examples of ultrasound, SONAR, and musical instruments.
